A Few Tricks for Wrapping localStorage So It Stops Annoying You
A few tricks for wrapping localStorage
When you're building web apps, you end up using
to store data all the time. The thing is, the
API itself isn't great to work with — so we can wrap it to make it nicer.

is a Web API the browser gives you. If you do web dev at all, you've definitely used it — it lets you store data in the browser, and that data sticks around even after you close the browser. There's also
, another browser Web API, but with that one the data disappears when the browser closes. So between these two Web APIs, you've got your options for storing data.
, since its whole deal is that the data survives closing the browser. That makes it good for stashing important stuff — login info, user preferences, that kind of thing.
works and is used exactly the same way as
— the only difference is its data vanishes when the browser closes, so it's better for less important stuff like temporary data. One interesting catch though:
shares data across different pages/tabs, but
doesn't. Makes sense — its data dies when the browser closes, so it can only share data within the same page.
, you can open your browser's dev tools, go to the Application tab, and find your new data under Local Storage — like this:
Every browser shows this in roughly the same way. I'm using Firefox, so yours might look slightly different, but it's close enough — go ahead and try it yourself.
API really is that simple, but it's got a few rough edges. What do I mean?
can actually store objects — for example...

But when you try to read it back with
Is that a bug? Do you need some special method to parse it? Nope — the reason is that
can only store strings. So when you want to store an object, you have to turn it into a string first:
And that's not the only gotcha. Like I just said, since
only stores strings, when you read data out you have to turn the string back into an object:
Which leads to yet another problem. If
and doesn't throw. But if your value is
JSON.parse(undefined) // Uncaught SyntaxError: JSON.parse: unexpected character at line 1 column 1 of the JSON data
// Uncaught SyntaxError: JSON.parse: unexpected character at line 1 column 1 of the JSON data
Or maybe you forgot to stringify the value when saving:

window.localStorage.setItem('myObj', { name: 'Ray', age: 18 })

JSON.parse(window.localStorage.getItem('myObj')) // Uncaught SyntaxError: JSON.parse: unexpected character at line 1 column 2 of the JSON data
// Uncaught SyntaxError: JSON.parse: unexpected character at line 1 column 2 of the JSON data
The quickest fix is to wrap it in
, or return an empty object when you hit
. But that makes the code kind of ugly, so instead let's solve it by wrapping localStorage in our own helper.

There are a bunch of ways to wrap it. Some people use a Class, some use a Function — I'll show both. Personally I lean toward the Function approach, so I'll start there and cover the Class version afterward.

'Missing the key to read from localStorage'
Now whenever someone forgets to pass
, they get a warning — that stops devs from forgetting it.
Then finally, when we read the value out, we should check too — if it's
You can return an empty object or an empty array — either works — but I prefer an empty object, because then you can just use
to check whether it's empty, like
. With an empty array you'd have to use
Then to use it, you just import

import { getStorageItem } from './utils/localStorage'

const myObj = getStorageItem('myObj')
See it? After wrapping, we don't have to call
ourselves anymore, plus we added some checks so devs won't forget to pass
— which avoids a bunch of silly mistakes.

Pretty simple, right? Wrapping really isn't hard, but it cuts down on a lot of silly mistakes — forgetting
, forgetting to stringify — all dodged by wrapping. And it keeps your code cleaner too.
There's one more advantage to wrapping. Once we've wrapped and imported it, we can check whether
even exists, and if it doesn't, warn the developer that the current environment doesn't support
'The current environment does not support localStorage'

When you're building web apps, you reach for localStorage to store data all the time — but the localStorage API itself is honestly kind of clunky. In this post I'll show a few tricks for wrapping it so it's nicer to use, covering both the Function and Class approaches, plus how to dodge the usual JSON.parse errors.
